name: Agile & delivery methodologies
description: Scrum, Kanban, XP and the SDLC models (Waterfall, V-model, iterative, DevOps, SAFe) — process reasoning for senior interviews. From the 'agile' and 'módszertanok' prep docs.

topics.name: Scrum
topics.description: Roles, events and artifacts of the Scrum framework; empirical process control.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Scrum is the most widely used agile framework: work happens in fixed-length sprints (1–4 weeks), with defined roles and a small set of recurring meetings, and every sprint ends with a usable increment of the product.

## What problem does it solve?

Long projects drift: requirements change, progress is invisible, feedback comes too late. Scrum forces a short cycle — plan a little, build a little, show it, adjust.

## How it works

Three roles:
- Product Owner: decides WHAT to build and in what order (owns the backlog).
- Scrum Master: keeps the process healthy, removes impediments — not a boss.
- Developers: decide HOW to build it.

The events in each sprint: sprint planning (choose what fits, set a sprint goal), daily scrum (15 minutes: what I did, what I will do, what blocks me), sprint review (show the working increment to stakeholders), retrospective (how WE worked — what to change next sprint).

The artifacts: product backlog (everything, ordered), sprint backlog (this sprint's slice), increment (the working result).

The engine underneath the ceremonies: two feedback loops per sprint — the review inspects the PRODUCT, the retro inspects the PROCESS. Skipping the retro means the team never improves — which is why "we skip the retro when busy" is a classic anti-pattern.

When Scrum fits: planned feature work with a stable team, where a protected sprint scope and a demo cadence help. Where it chafes: interrupt-driven work (support, ops) where the sprint plan is fiction by Tuesday — that is Kanban territory.

## Common mistakes

- Daily standup as a status report to a manager
- Sprint scope changed mid-sprint by the PO (new work → next sprint)
- Skipping retros "when busy" — improvement is the first casualty
- The Scrum Master as team boss instead of impediment remover

## Key terms

- Sprint — a fixed timebox producing a usable increment
- Product Owner / Scrum Master / Developers — what / how-we-work / how
- Sprint goal — the one sentence the sprint commits to
- Review vs retrospective — inspect the product vs inspect the process

topics.name: Kanban
topics.description: Flow-based method: visualize work, limit WIP, manage flow.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Kanban is a way of managing work as a continuous FLOW instead of in sprints: visualize all work on a board, limit how much is in progress at once, and measure how fast items travel.

## What problem does it solve?

For interrupt-driven work — support, operations, maintenance — sprint plans die on contact with reality. Kanban has no sprints to break: work is pulled continuously as capacity frees up.

## How it works

Three practices:
- Visualize: every piece of work is a card on a board (To Do → In Progress → Review → Done). Invisible work cannot be managed.
- Limit WIP: each column has a maximum ("max 3 in progress"). This is the engine of the method.
- Measure flow: lead time (request → done), cycle time (started → done), throughput (items per week).

Why WIP limits work — the interview question: less parallel work means less context switching; queues become visible instead of hiding inside "in progress"; finishing gets prioritized over starting (you PULL new work only when a slot frees); and a blocked item visibly clogs its column, forcing the team to swarm on it. Counterintuitively, limiting work in progress INCREASES total throughput.

Scrum vs Kanban is not a religion: Scrum gives cadence and commitment (good for planned product work); Kanban gives flow and flexibility (good for continuous/reactive work). Many teams mix them ("Scrumban") — e.g. Scrum's ceremonies with WIP limits on the board.

## Common mistakes

- A board with no WIP limits — that is a todo list, not Kanban
- Starting new items while blocked ones rot (pull, don't push)
- No flow measurement — lead/cycle time unknown, so no improvement

## Key terms

- WIP limit — the cap on parallel work; the core mechanism
- Pull system — take new work only when capacity frees
- Lead time / cycle time — request-to-done / started-to-done
- Cumulative flow diagram — the chart that reveals bottlenecks

topics.name: SDLC models
topics.description: Waterfall, V-model, iterative/incremental, spiral and when each applies.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The classic software development lifecycle models — waterfall, V-model, iterative, spiral, agile — and the honest logic for choosing between them.

## What problem does it solve?

Different projects carry different risks. A regulated medical device and a startup MVP should NOT be run the same way; the models are different answers to "how much do we plan up front?".

## How it works

- Waterfall: sequential phases — requirements → design → implementation → testing → release — each finished before the next begins. Works when requirements are truly fixed; painful otherwise, because change late in the sequence is very expensive.
- V-model: waterfall bent into a V — every specification level on the left has a matching TEST level on the right (requirements ↔ acceptance tests, design ↔ integration tests). Its spec-to-test traceability is exactly what regulated industries audit.
- Iterative/incremental: build in slices, learn between slices.
- Spiral: iterations explicitly driven by RISK analysis — each loop attacks the biggest remaining risk.
- Agile: short cycles, evolving requirements, continuous feedback.

The selection logic: it depends on requirement STABILITY and the COST OF LATE CHANGE. Fixed, well-understood, contract-bound requirements — safety-critical software, fixed-bid tenders with heavy audit demands — genuinely favor plan-driven models. Evolving requirements and product discovery favor agile, whose short feedback loops keep late change cheap.

The mature closing point: real organizations run hybrids — plan-driven governance at the milestone level with agile delivery inside, or agile development with a formal verification phase where regulation demands it. Naming a credible hybrid beats declaring one model universally right.

## Common mistakes

- "Agile is always better" — regulated/fixed-bid contexts genuinely favor plan-driven
- Waterfall phases hidden inside "sprints" (design sprint, dev sprint, test sprint)
- Ignoring the cost-of-late-change question that actually decides the model

## Key terms

- Waterfall — sequential, plan-everything-first
- V-model — every spec level paired with a test level
- Spiral — risk-driven iterations
- Cost of late change — the variable that drives the choice

topics.name: DevOps
topics.description: Culture and practices uniting dev and ops: CI/CD, automation, shared ownership.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

DevOps is a way of working where the people who write the software and the people who run it in production work as one team, with shared responsibility. The name comes from Development + Operations.

## What problem does it solve?

Traditionally, developers wrote code and handed it to a separate operations team to deploy and run. This handover caused slow releases, finger-pointing when something broke ("works on my machine" / "your code crashed my server"), and developers who never learned how their code behaves in production. DevOps removes the handover: the team that builds it also runs it.

## How it works

The practices that make it real:
- CI/CD pipeline: every code change is automatically built, tested and made deployable. Releasing becomes routine — many small deploys instead of one scary quarterly event.
- Infrastructure as code: servers and environments are defined in version-controlled files, so they can be recreated exactly — no hand-configured, unrepeatable machines.
- Monitoring and alerting: the team watches how its software behaves in production and can react — or roll back — within minutes.
- Shared ownership: "you build it, you run it" — the team carries the pager for its own services. This changes incentives: writing operable, well-monitored code becomes self-interest.

How to tell if it is working — the four DORA metrics: how often you deploy, how long a change takes to reach production, what fraction of deploys fail, and how fast you recover. If those numbers improve, DevOps is working; buying tools without moving them is theater.

## Common mistakes

- Buying tools and declaring DevOps while the dev/ops wall stands
- A separate "DevOps team" that becomes the new ops silo
- No DORA-style measurement — nobody can say if anything improved
- Quarterly big-bang releases surviving under a CI/CD banner

## Key terms

- CI/CD — automated build, test and deploy of every change
- Infrastructure as code — environments from versioned files, not clicks
- You build it, you run it — the ownership principle
- DORA metrics — deploy frequency, lead time, failure rate, recovery time
- Rollback — fast, practiced return to the previous version

topics.name: Agile Manifesto, User Stories & Estimation
topics.description: The four values and twelve principles; INVEST stories, acceptance criteria, story points, planning poker, velocity.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The foundations under every agile method: the Agile Manifesto's values, and the everyday craft of user stories, estimation and velocity.

## What problem does it solve?

Teams that adopt agile ceremonies without the underlying values end up doing "waterfall with standups". The Manifesto is the why; stories and estimation are the daily how.

## How it works

The Manifesto (2001) states four values, each "we value X over Y" — where Y still matters, X just wins when they conflict: individuals and interactions over processes and tools; working software over comprehensive documentation; customer collaboration over contract negotiation; responding to change over following a plan. Its twelve principles boil down to: deliver small and often, welcome change, measure progress by working software, keep a sustainable pace, care about technical quality, keep it simple (YAGNI), trust self-organizing teams, and regularly reflect and adjust.

User stories describe work from the user's point of view: "As a [role], I want [capability], so that [value]." A good story passes INVEST — Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small enough for a sprint, Testable. Acceptance criteria (Given/When/Then, including error cases) define exactly when it is done.

Story points measure RELATIVE size — complexity + effort + uncertainty — deliberately NOT time. Teams use a Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21) with reference anchors: 1 = tiny fix, 5 = complete small feature with tests, 13 = a payment integration. Too big to estimate → split it, or run a spike (a small research task) first.

Planning poker prevents the loudest voice from anchoring everyone: each person picks a card in secret, all reveal simultaneously, the extremes explain their reasoning, then re-vote.

Velocity — the team's average completed points over recent sprints — exists for ONE purpose: knowing how much to commit to next sprint. Using it to compare teams (points are relative, per-team) or as a performance KPI (invites point inflation) are the classic abuses.

## Common mistakes

- Story points converted to hours in a spreadsheet
- Velocity compared across teams or pushed as a KPI → point inflation
- Stories without acceptance criteria — "done" becomes an argument
- Estimating aloud in turn — anchoring; poker exists for a reason

## Key terms

- INVEST — the checklist for a good user story
- Acceptance criteria — Given/When/Then, error cases included
- Story point — relative size, never hours
- Planning poker — simultaneous secret votes against anchoring
- Velocity — a planning input, never a KPI

topics.name: Extreme Programming & Lean
topics.description: XP's technical practices (TDD, pairing, CI, refactoring) and Lean's waste-elimination principles.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Two schools that supplied agile's engineering half: Extreme Programming (XP) — the technical practices like TDD and pair programming — and Lean, the waste-elimination mindset borrowed from Toyota manufacturing.

## What problem does it solve?

Scrum says nothing about HOW to write code. Teams doing Scrum without engineering discipline accumulate bugs and technical debt sprint after sprint. XP fills that gap; Lean explains which activities deserve to exist at all.

## How it works

XP's practices, the ones that outlived the name:
- TDD — the red → green → refactor loop: write a small FAILING test first (red), write the minimal code that passes (green), clean the code up while the tests stay green (refactor), repeat. The tests accumulate into a safety net that makes future change safe.
- Pair programming — two people, one machine: a driver typing, a navigator thinking ahead. Fewer bugs, and knowledge spreads automatically.
- Continuous integration — merge, build and test several times a DAY, so integration pain never accumulates.
- Continuous refactoring, collective code ownership (no "my file"), coding standards, small frequent releases, sustainable pace.

The combo most real teams run: Scrum for the frame (sprints, roles, ceremonies) + XP for the engineering inside it.

Lean (adapted from the Toyota Production System) has one central idea: anything that does not add value to the customer is WASTE. Its software waste list is uncomfortably recognizable: features nobody uses, half-finished work (WIP), waiting (for reviews, approvals, environments), needless process, context switching between projects, defects (the later found, the costlier), and manual work that should be automated. Kanban's WIP limits and DevOps automation are both direct attacks on this list.

## Common mistakes

- TDD reduced to "we write tests… afterwards"
- Pairing mandated 100% of the time or dismissed 100% — both dogma
- "Refactoring sprints" instead of continuous cleanup
- Waste elimination pushed until documentation disappears

## Key terms

- Red → green → refactor — the TDD cycle
- Pair programming — driver + navigator
- Continuous integration — merge and verify many times a day
- Waste — anything not adding customer value
- Build quality in — prevent defects instead of finding them late

topics.name: Agile Metrics, Scaling & Anti-patterns
topics.description: Burndown/burnup, lead/cycle time, CFD; Scrum of Scrums, LeSS, SAFe PI Planning; the classic failure modes.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The charts agile teams steer by (burndown, burnup, cumulative flow), the frameworks for coordinating MANY teams on one product (Scrum of Scrums, LeSS, SAFe), and the catalogue of ways agile goes wrong.

## What problem does it solve?

One team's Scrum does not answer "are we on track?", "how do 8 teams coordinate?", or "why does our agile feel like theater?". These are those answers.

## How it works

Metrics:
- Burndown chart: remaining work through the sprint vs an ideal line — above the line means the sprint goal is at risk.
- Burnup chart: completed work climbing toward a separate total-scope line. Its advantage: scope CHANGES are visible instead of masking progress.
- Flow metrics (Kanban side): lead time, cycle time, throughput, and the cumulative flow diagram — where a widening band exposes the bottleneck column.
- Escaped defects: bugs found in production — a quality signal for any method. All of these are diagnostics; turning them into targets corrupts them.

Scaling, smallest hammer first:
- Scrum of Scrums: each team sends a representative to a short coordination standup a few times a week — dependencies, impediments, plans.
- LeSS: several teams, ONE product backlog, ONE Product Owner — deliberately minimal extra process.
- SAFe: the heavyweight for 50–125+ people — release trains and quarterly PI Planning, a two-day all-hands event where every team plans the next ~5 sprints, maps cross-team dependencies on a program board, and votes confidence (average under 3 of 5 → re-plan). Adopt it only at genuine organizational scale.

Anti-patterns to recognize: "agile in name only" (waterfall with standups); "Scrum-but" (Scrum minus the retro/PO/review — it is a package, not a menu); velocity pressure from management (→ point inflation); scope creep mid-sprint (new work belongs in the NEXT sprint); chronic carry-over (stories too big); skipped retros; 45-minute standups; and on the technical side: no CI, no tests, big-bang integration at sprint end.

## Common mistakes

- SAFe rolled out for three teams — Scrum of Scrums would have done
- Burndown watched while scope silently grows (burnup shows it)
- Metrics turned into targets — they stop being measurements
- Cross-team dependencies discovered mid-sprint instead of at planning

## Key terms

- Burnup vs burndown — scope changes visible vs hidden
- Cumulative flow diagram — the bottleneck finder
- Scrum of Scrums / LeSS / SAFe — light → medium → heavy scaling
- PI Planning — SAFe's quarterly all-teams planning event
- Scrum-but — cherry-picked Scrum that quietly stops working

questions.title: Scrum vs Kanban
questions.prompt: What is the difference between Scrum and Kanban, and how would you choose between them for a team?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cadence/sprints and roles vs continuous flow and WIP limits; which fits planned work vs interrupt-driven work.

expectedPoints.label: Scrum = timeboxed sprints, roles, ceremonies
expectedPoints.description: Fixed-length sprints, defined roles and events, commitment per sprint.

expectedPoints.label: Kanban = continuous flow with WIP limits
expectedPoints.description: Continuous pull, visualize the board, limit work in progress, optimize flow.

expectedPoints.label: Matches method to the work
expectedPoints.description: Scrum for planned feature work in a stable team; Kanban for interrupt-driven/support/ops work.

followUps.prompt: Why do WIP limits actually improve throughput?

questions.title: Agile vs Waterfall
questions.prompt: When would a waterfall or V-model approach be a better fit than agile?
questions.answerStructureHint: Fixed vs evolving requirements, regulatory/contractual constraints, cost of late change.

expectedPoints.label: Depends on requirement stability
expectedPoints.description: Waterfall fits fixed, well-understood requirements; agile fits evolving ones.

expectedPoints.label: Regulatory / contractual / safety context
expectedPoints.description: Heavy documentation, audits, safety-critical or fixed-bid contracts favor plan-driven models.

expectedPoints.label: Cost of late change
expectedPoints.description: Waterfall makes late changes expensive; agile keeps feedback loops short to reduce that risk.

followUps.prompt: In practice teams often mix these. What does a pragmatic hybrid look like?

questions.title: What DevOps actually means
questions.prompt: What does DevOps mean beyond tooling, and what practices make it work?
questions.answerStructureHint: Shared ownership culture, CI/CD automation, fast feedback, observability — not just a job title.

expectedPoints.label: Culture of shared ownership
expectedPoints.description: Breaks the dev/ops wall; teams own their software in production ('you build it, you run it').
acceptedSignals: you build it you run it

expectedPoints.label: CI/CD and automation
expectedPoints.description: Automated pipelines: build, test, deploy; infrastructure as code.

expectedPoints.label: Fast feedback and observability
expectedPoints.description: Monitoring, quick rollbacks and short feedback loops to production.

followUps.prompt: How would you measure whether a team's DevOps is actually improving?

questions.title: Story points and planning poker
questions.prompt: How does story-point estimation work, what makes a good user story, and what should velocity be used for — and not used for?

expectedPoints.label: Points are relative, not time
expectedPoints.description: Complexity + effort + uncertainty on a Fibonacci scale with reference stories.

expectedPoints.label: Poker prevents anchoring
expectedPoints.description: Secret simultaneous votes; extremes explain; re-vote to consensus.

expectedPoints.label: Velocity for planning only
expectedPoints.description: 3–5 sprint average guides commitment; never compare teams or push it as a KPI.

questions.title: TDD's red-green-refactor
questions.prompt: Describe the TDD cycle and the XP practices you would bring into any team — and where Lean's 'waste' idea connects.

expectedPoints.label: Red → green → refactor
expectedPoints.description: Failing test first, minimal pass, cleanup with green tests; no code without a test.

expectedPoints.label: XP practice set
expectedPoints.description: Pairing, CI several times daily, continuous refactoring, collective ownership, small releases.

expectedPoints.label: Lean waste connection
expectedPoints.description: Unused features, WIP, waiting, task switching, manual work — practices exist to eliminate waste.

questions.title: Coordinating multiple agile teams
questions.prompt: Three teams now work on one product. How do you coordinate them, which metrics do you watch, and which anti-patterns do you guard against?

expectedPoints.label: Right-sized scaling mechanism
expectedPoints.description: Scrum of Scrums ambassadors first; LeSS one-backlog-one-PO; SAFe/PI Planning only at real scale.

expectedPoints.label: Flow metrics + honest charts
expectedPoints.description: Lead/cycle time, throughput, CFD for bottlenecks; burnup keeps scope changes visible.

expectedPoints.label: Names concrete anti-patterns
expectedPoints.description: Velocity comparison/pressure, scope creep, skipped retros, agile-in-name-only.
